votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq logic, using zip to count differences and correctly slicing the array to compare the prefix and reversed suffix.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, using zip to count differences and slicing to extract the prefix and reversed suffix.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, using zip to count differences up to the length of the shorter list and correctly slicing the prefix and reversed suffix.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. count_diffs iterates through pairs using zip (which stops at the shorter list, matching Coq's behavior), counting differences. smallest_change_spec computes half_len as integer division, takes the first half_len elements as prefix, reverses the array and takes the first half_len elements as suffix_reversed (equivalent to Coq's firstn half_len (rev arr)), then checks if cnt equals the count of differences. Both implementations produce the same concrete results.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. count_diffs iterates through pairs using zip (which stops at the shorter list, matching Coq's behavior), counting mismatches. smallest_change_spec computes half_len as integer division, takes the first half_len elements as prefix, reverses the array and takes the first half_len elements as suffix_reversed (equivalent to Coq's firstn half_len (rev arr)), and checks if cnt equals the count of differences. Both implementations produce the same concrete results.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. count_diffs iterates through pairs using zip (which stops at the shorter list, matching Coq's behavior), counting mismatches. smallest_change_spec computes half_len as integer division, takes the first half_len elements as prefix, reverses the array and takes the first half_len elements as suffix_reversed (equivalent to Coq's firstn half_len (rev arr)), and checks if cnt equals the count of differences. Both implementations produce the same concrete results.

Python zip-based count matches Coq recursion stopping at shorter list; integer division, prefix/suffix-reversed selection, and mismatch counting align. Equality check corresponds to the Coq Prop condition on all concrete inputs.
Both compute half length, compare prefix with reversed suffix pairwise up to min length, and count mismatches; Python zip logic matches Coq recursion and equality semantics for integers.

Both compute the number of mismatches between the first half of the list and the first half of the reversed list (i.e., mirrored pairs), using floor(length/2) and pairing only up to the shorter list; Python’s zip-based count matches the Coq recursion on all inputs.
